title: Checkout Conversion Drop
problem_statement: Completed orders have dropped across key Indian metros since mid-January while competitors hold steady. Leadership needs a data-backed root cause and a recovery plan.

problems.title: Order Drop Investigation
problems.challenge_prompt: Completed orders have dropped across key Indian metros since mid-January. Competitors are holding steady. Find what's causing the decline and propose a recovery plan.
problems.challenge_problem_statement: ZaikaNow, an India-native food delivery marketplace, has seen a significant drop in completed orders starting mid-January across Bengaluru, Mumbai, and Delhi NCR. Leadership is concerned — major competitors aren't showing the same decline. Your job is to investigate the root cause using data from analytics, engineering, and user research, then propose an actionable recovery plan.
problems.root_cause: The RupeeFlow v3 payment migration (DEP004) on January 10, 2025 broke UPI callback confirmation handling and degraded overall payment processing. UPI users were hit hardest (29-point success rate drop vs ~15 for other methods). Since UPI represents 60%+ of transactions, this caused a ~40% drop in completed orders. A Feb 1 hotfix partially restored reliability, but trust damage persists — order volumes remain 25-30% below baseline.
key_evidence: Daily completed orders drop ~40% on Jan 10 — sharp break unrelated to Christmas promo ending Dec 26
Funnel break is at payment_attempt → order_complete; traffic and cart adds are UP
UPI success rate drops 29 points (82% → 53%); other methods drop ~15 points
DEP004: RupeeFlow v3 migration deployed Jan 10 at 06:00
Payment service p99 latency spikes 5x (495ms → 2,421ms); error rate jumps 8x
Top 5 error codes include 3 UPI-specific: UPI_CALLBACK_TIMEOUT, UPI_COLLECT_PENDING, TRANSACTION_REVERSED
Usability study: task completion dropped from 93% to 61%; users report money debited without confirmation
Post-hotfix (Feb 1): orders improve but remain 25-30% below baseline — trust damage persists
red_herrings_to_reject: Christmas promo normalization (resolves by Dec 28, 13-day gap before payment failures start Jan 10)
Search service p99 spike (Jan 8, resolves immediately, unrelated to checkout)
Republic Day promo (late Jan, merchandizing not payments)
Notification service errors (Jan 20-22, communication channel, not payment flow)
Checkout success animation (Jan 10, cosmetic change, insufficient scale)

data_model.schema_notes: MECE design: city, platform, and user_type live in the users table only. Other tables reference users via FK (user_id). To analyze orders by city or platform, JOIN orders to users.

analyst.persona: You are Priya, a pragmatic senior data analyst. You investigate trends, cohorts, customer behavior, payments, funnels, and business performance with SQL and careful evidence synthesis.

ux_researcher.persona: You are Kavya, a UX researcher focused on user pain points, behavioral friction, support signals, review themes, and product experience gaps.

engineering_lead.persona: You are Rohan, an engineering lead who investigates incidents, regressions, deployments, service health, error patterns, and rollout risk using operational data.

analyst.description: Business metrics, funnels, payment method analysis, cohort segmentation. JOIN orders to users for city/platform/user_type dimensions.

ux_researcher.description: User feedback, support tickets, usability research, and product change history. JOIN reviews/tickets to users for city/platform context.

engineering_lead.description: Deployment history, service health metrics, architecture notes, and detailed error log with per-error tracing across services.

expected_key_findings: A sharp drop in completed orders (~40%) begins Jan 10 — not related to Christmas promo ending Dec 26
The funnel break is specifically at payment_attempt → order_complete; top-of-funnel traffic is UP
UPI is the most affected payment method — 29-point success rate drop (82% → 53%) vs ~15 points for others
UPI represents 60%+ of transactions, making it the primary driver of the order drop
RupeeFlow v3 migration (DEP004) deployed Jan 10 at 06:00 aligns exactly with the degradation
Payment service p99 latency spikes 5x (495ms → 2,421ms) on Jan 10; error rate jumps 8x
Top error codes are UPI-specific: UPI_CALLBACK_TIMEOUT, UPI_COLLECT_PENDING, TRANSACTION_REVERSED
Usability study confirms user-facing impact: task completion dropped 93% → 61%
Feb 1 hotfix partially recovers orders but volumes remain 25-30% below baseline
Trust damage persists — users who experienced failures order less or switched to competitors

red_herrings.signal: Christmas campaign caused a visible demand spike from December 20-26
red_herrings.why_misleading: Traffic normalization after the campaign resolves by Dec 28, a full two weeks before the payment-step degradation that starts on January 10.

red_herrings.signal: Search service p99 latency spikes on January 8
red_herrings.why_misleading: The spike is brief, resolves quickly, and does not line up with the checkout failure pattern.

red_herrings.signal: Republic Day homepage banner launches in late January
red_herrings.why_misleading: A merchandising change, not a technical one — the reliability break is in payments, not discovery.

red_herrings.signal: Notification service errors rise briefly on January 20-22
red_herrings.why_misleading: This creates noise in communication flows, not in payment authorization or confirmation.

red_herrings.signal: Checkout success animation ships on January 10
red_herrings.why_misleading: Adjacent in time but too minor to explain the scale and persistence of the payment degradation.

levels.excellent: Identifies the RupeeFlow v3 migration as the root cause, explains UPI as the primary affected method (29-point drop), and recognizes that trust damage persists after the hotfix
levels.good: Identifies the payment gateway issue and the UPI/city pattern, but may not fully explain the post-fix trust damage
levels.adequate: Identifies the payment step as the main problem but does not isolate the migration or affected segment cleanly
levels.poor: Blames weaker factors such as promos ending, search ranking, or general traffic decline

levels.excellent: Systematically queries all three agents, cross-references evidence across domains, rejects red herrings with reasoning, and builds a coherent timeline
levels.good: Uses multiple agents and connects the main technical and UX evidence
levels.adequate: Explores some evidence but misses important cross-functional links or timeline logic
levels.poor: Relies on a single agent or reaches a conclusion without sufficient evidence

levels.excellent: Proposes immediate stabilization, payment-status UX fixes, customer trust recovery for affected cohorts, and monitoring for repeat-order recovery
levels.good: Proposes strong technical remediation plus some customer recovery actions
levels.adequate: Suggests fixes for payment reliability but lacks user-trust or monitoring depth
levels.poor: Offers generic actions without prioritization or evidence linkage

levels.excellent: Frames the problem for leadership with business impact, affected segments, customer trust risk, and a clear recovery narrative
levels.good: Provides a clear summary with supporting evidence
levels.adequate: Explains the issue but with limited prioritization or synthesis
levels.poor: Communicates in a fragmented way or dumps disconnected observations
